document: Joint Standard 1 of 2023 - IT Governance and Risk Management

sections.heading: Definitions and interpretation
sections.summary: Defines key terms including financial institutions, Authorities, fit and proper, and IT-related concepts for the Joint Standard.

sections.heading: Application
sections.summary: Specifies that financial institutions must mitigate IT risks for all related entities, including foreign branches and subsidiaries, and align requirements with their risk profile.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must address IT risks for all local and foreign entities under their control, including subsidiaries, to ensure compliance with the Joint Standard.

requirements.text: A financial institution that is a bank, or a controlling company must ensure that any risks relating to IT risk from juristic persons and branches structured under the bank or the controlling company (both local and foreign), including all relevant subsidiaries approved in terms of section 52 of the Banks Act, 1990, are catered for and mitigated in the application of the requirements of this Joint Standard.

A financial institution that is an insurer or a controlling company of an insurance group must ensure that any risks relating to IT from juristic persons structured under the insurer or the controlling company (both local and foreign) and from the insurance group designated under section 10 of the Insurance Act, 2017 are catered for and mitigated in the application of the requirements of this Joint Standard.

sections.source_quote: 4.1 This Joint Standard applies to financial institutions as defined.

sections.heading: Roles and responsibilities
sections.summary: Governing body is ultimately responsible for IT compliance, must establish a sound IT risk management framework, and define roles and responsibilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Governing body must ensure IT risk management framework and define roles for IT risk oversight.

requirements.text: The governing body is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the financial institution complies with the requirements as set out in this Joint Standard.
The governing body must ensure, together with senior management, that a sound and robust IT risk management framework and IT strategy is established and maintained.

The governing body must clearly define the roles and responsibilities of all management, execution, oversight and control functions as well as committees established for the purpose of exercising oversight of IT risks.

sections.source_quote: 5.1 The governing body is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the financial institution complies with the requirements as set out in this Joint Standard.

sections.heading: IT strategy
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have IT strategy approved by governing body, aligned with business strategy, and reviewed annually.
sections.dtia_summary: IT strategy must be approved by governing body, aligned with business strategy, and reviewed at least annually.

requirements.text: A financial institution must ensure that its IT strategy is approved by the governing body and aligned with its overall business strategy.
The IT strategy of a financial institution must be reviewed regularly, but at least annually, in consideration of market, industry, technology and other relevant developments.

sections.source_quote: 6.1 A financial institution must ensure that its IT strategy is approved by the governing body and aligned with its overall business strategy.

sections.heading: IT risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish an IT risk management framework approved by governing body, incorporating policies, risk identification, and monitoring processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must establish an IT risk management framework that includes risk identification, assessment, and monitoring processes.

requirements.text: A financial institution must establish an IT risk management framework to manage IT risks in a systematic and consistent manner.
The IT risk management framework of a financial institution must be approved by the governing body and reviewed regularly, but at least annually.
The IT risk management framework of a financial institution must, at a minimum, incorporate policies, standards and procedures in managing IT risks and safeguarding IT assets.
The IT risk management framework must include identification and prioritisation of IT assets.
The IT risk management framework must include identification and assessment of impact and likelihood of current and emerging threats, risks and vulnerabilities.
The IT risk management framework must include implementation of appropriate practices and controls to manage risks.
The IT risk management framework must include periodic updates and monitoring of risk assessments.
The IT risk management framework must include people management measures for staff, service providers and contractors.

sections.source_quote: 7.1 A financial institution must establish an IT risk management framework to manage IT risks in a systematic and consistent manner.

sections.heading: Oversight of IT risk management
sections.summary: Oversight of IT risk management must be incorporated into governance structures and reporting lines to the governing body.
sections.dtia_summary: Oversight of IT risk management must be part of governance structures with reporting lines to the governing body.

requirements.text: Oversight of IT risk management must be incorporated into the governance and risk management structures, processes and procedures of a financial institution, including provisions relating to reporting lines to the governing body.

sections.source_quote: 8.1 Oversight of IT risk management must be incorporated into the governance and risk management structures, processes and procedures of a financial institution, including provisions relating to reporting lines to the governing body.

sections.heading: IT operations
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust IT service management policies, change management, incident handling, and backup procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must implement IT service management policies, change management, incident handling, and backup procedures.

requirements.text: A financial institution must develop a robust set of IT service management policies, standards, processes and procedures (framework) which is essential for supporting IT systems, services and operations, managing changes, incidents and problems as well as ensuring the stability of the production IT environment.
The IT service management framework must comprise a governance structure for change management, software release management, incident and problem management as well as capacity management.
A financial institution must manage its IT operations based on documented and implemented policies, processes and procedures.

A financial institution must maintain efficiency of its IT operations, including, but not limited to the need to consider how to minimise potential incidents arising from the execution of manual tasks.
A financial institution must implement appropriate logging and monitoring procedures for critical IT operations to allow the detection, analysis and correction of incidents.
A financial institution must store the configuration of the IT assets and the links and interdependencies between the different IT assets, to enable an appropriate configuration management process.

A financial institution must implement performance, capacity planning and monitoring processes to prevent, detect and respond to important performance issues of IT systems and IT capacity shortages in a timely manner.
A financial institution must define and implement IT system backup and restoration procedures to ensure recovery of IT systems as required.

A financial institution must establish and implement an effective IT change management process to ensure that all changes to IT systems are recorded, tested, assessed, approved, implemented and verified in a controlled manner.

A financial institution must establish and implement a problem and incident management process to identify, track (including timing), log, categorise and classify incidents according to priority, based on business criticality.

sections.source_quote: 9.1 A financial institution must develop a robust set of IT service management policies, standards, processes and procedures (framework) which is essential for supporting IT systems, services and operations, managing changes, incidents and problems as well as ensuring the stability of the production IT environment.

sections.heading: Handling of sensitive or confidential information
sections.summary: Financial institutions must protect sensitive information, implement access controls, and ensure data accuracy and compliance with legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must protect sensitive information, implement access controls, and ensure data accuracy and compliance with legislation.

requirements.text: A financial institution must define, document and implement appropriate measures to protect sensitive or confidential information such as customer personal account and transaction data.
A financial institution must define, document and implement procedures for logical access control (identity and access management).
A financial institution must implement appropriate measures to address risks of data theft, data loss and data leakage from endpoint devices, customer service locations and call centres.
A financial institution must ensure that information processed, stored or transmitted between itself and its customers is accurate, reliable and complete.
A financial institution must conduct independent reviews to assess compliance with the measures implemented.

A financial institution must ensure that all personal information is processed in accordance with the requirements of all applicable legislation, including the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013.

sections.source_quote: 10.1 A financial institution must define, document and implement appropriate measures to - (a) protect sensitive or confidential information such as customer personal account and transaction data which are stored and processed in systems; and (b) mitigate IT risks and protect information assets in accordance with its sensitivity classification.

sections.heading: Risks associated with financial products and financial services
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify IT risks for financial products/services and implement security controls, system availability, and recovery capabilities proportional to risk exposure.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to assess IT risks for financial products/services and implement proportional security controls, system availability, and recovery capabilities.

requirements.text: A financial institution must clearly identify IT risks associated with the types of financial products or financial services being offered, and formulate security controls, system availability and recovery capabilities, which are commensurate with the level of risk exposure for all operations, including the internet-facing operations.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must clearly identify IT risks associated with the types of financial products or financial services being offered, and formulate security controls, system availability and recovery capabilities, which are commensurate with the level of risk exposure for all operations, including the internet-facing operations.

sections.heading: IT programme and/or project management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must develop IT programme/project management frameworks with governance, risk management, and change control processes, ensuring security requirements are independent and approved.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to establish IT programme/project management frameworks with governance, risk assessment, and change control, ensuring security requirements are independent and approved.

requirements.text: A financial institution must develop a framework and approach for IT programme and/or project management that incorporates the governance structures, policies and processes, stakeholder engagement, risks and issues management, change control, integration, and cost and benefit realisation.

A financial institution must establish and implement an IT programme and project management policies, procedures and processes that includes, as a minimum - IT programme and project objectives; roles and responsibilities, including governance and decision-making structures; IT programme and project risk assessment; IT programme and project plan, timeframe and steps; key milestones; and change management requirements.

A financial institution must ensure that its IT programme and project management policy confirms that IT security requirements are analysed and approved by a function that is independent from the development function.

A financial institution must identify, monitor and mitigate risks deriving from its portfolio of IT programmes and projects, considering risks that may result from interdependencies between different IT programmes and projects and from dependencies of multiple programmes and projects utilising the same resources and/or expertise.

A financial institution must ensure that before any acquisition or development of IT systems takes place, the functional and non-functional requirements (including information security requirements) are clearly defined and approved by the relevant governance structure.

A financial institution must follow its methodology for testing and approval of IT systems prior to implementation into the production environment. This methodology must consider the criticality of business processes and assets. The testing must ensure that new IT systems perform as intended. It must also use test environments that adequately reflect the production environment.

A financial institution must implement controls in the IT environment to ensure adequate segregation of duties between the pre-production environment that is a mirror of the production environment to mitigate the impact of risks introduced to the production systems.

A financial institution must implement appropriate measures to protect the integrity of the source codes of IT systems that are developed in-house. In addition, a financial institution must document the development, implementation, operation and/or configuration of the IT systems comprehensively to reduce any unnecessary dependency on subject matter experts.
A financial institution must ensure that the documentation of the IT system contains, where applicable, user documentation, technical system documentation and operating procedures.

A financial institution must ensure that processes for acquisition and development of IT systems applied by the department(s) responsible for IT must also apply to IT systems acquired by business functions outside the IT department, using a risk-based approach.
A financial institution must maintain a register of the critical applications, business functions and processes.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must develop a framework and approach for IT programme and/or project management that incorporates the governance structures, policies and processes, stakeholder engagement, risks and issues management, change control, integration, and cost and benefit realisation.

sections.heading: IT resilience and business continuity
sections.summary: Financial institutions must define recovery priorities, establish disaster recovery sites, implement IT resilience plans, and conduct business impact assessments for critical business functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to define recovery priorities, establish disaster recovery sites, implement IT resilience plans, and conduct business impact assessments for critical business functions.

requirements.text: A financial institution must define system recovery and business resumption priorities and establish specific Service Level Objectives including RTOs and RPOs for critical services and business processes.

A financial institution must identify and establish a disaster recovery site(s) that is geographically separate from the primary site to enable the recovery of critical systems and continuation of business operations, should a disruption occur at the primary site.

A financial institution must establish a sound IT resilience management process to maximise its abilities to provide services on an ongoing basis and to limit losses in the event of severe business disruption in line with any existing requirements issued in terms of a financial sector law and applicable to financial institutions.

A financial institution must conduct a business impact assessment by analysing its exposure to severe business disruptions and assessing its potential impacts (including on confidentiality, integrity and availability), quantitatively and qualitatively, using internal and/or external data and scenario analysis.

A business impact assessment referred to in paragraph 13.2 above must also consider the criticality of the identified and classified business functions, supporting processes, third parties and IT assets, and their interdependencies.
A financial institution must develop a sound IT resilience plan.
The IT resilience plan must specifically consider risks that could adversely impact IT systems and services.

The IT resilience plan must support the objective to protect and, if necessary, re-establish the confidentiality, integrity and availability of its business functions, supporting processes and IT assets.
The IT resilience plan must support critical business functions, business processes, IT assets and its interdependencies (including those provided by third parties, where applicable).
The IT resilience plan must cater for processes to enable the return to a state of normality in the event of severe business disruption.

The IT resilience plan must be aligned to the business impact assessment referred to in paragraph 13.2 above, for example, with redundancy of certain critical components to prevent disruptions caused by events impacting those components.

The IT resilience plan must be tested regularly, but at least annually. Various scenarios, including total shutdown or incapacitation of the primary site as well as component failure at the individual system or application cluster level, must be covered in IT resilience tests.

The IT resilience plan must be reviewed regularly, but at least annually. The review must be based on, amongst others, testing results, current threat intelligence and lessons learnt from previous events.

To achieve data centre resilience, a financial institution must assess the redundancy and fault tolerance in areas such as electrical power, air conditioning, fire suppression and data communications, to the extent applicable.

A financial institution must define, document and implement physical security measures to protect its premises, data centres and sensitive areas from unauthorised access and from environmental hazards.

A financial institution must test the recovery dependencies between systems. Bilateral or multilateral recovery testing must be conducted where networks and systems are linked to specific service providers, where applicable.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must define system recovery and business resumption priorities and establish specific Service Level Objectives including RTOs and RPOs for critical services and business processes.

sections.heading: IT assurance
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish independent IT assurance functions to review compliance with IT policies and procedures and external requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to establish independent IT assurance functions to review compliance with IT policies and procedures and external requirements.

requirements.text: The control functions and/or external assurance providers, must, have the capacity to independently review and provide objective assurance of compliance with all IT-related activities as outlined in the financial institution's policies and procedures as well as with external requirements.

A financial institution must through the control functions or an external assurance provider - establish an organisational structure and reporting lines for IT assurance within the control functions, where appropriate, in a way that preserves the independence and objectivity of the control functions.

A financial institution must through the control functions or an external assurance provider - determine whether changes in the existing operational environment influence the existing IT controls or require the adoption of additional measures to mitigate the risks involved. These changes must be in accordance with the financial institution's formal change management process.

A financial institution must through the control functions or an external assurance provider - maintain an IT assurance plan to examine and evaluate the adequacy and effectiveness of the financial institution's IT systems, internal control mechanisms and governance arrangements.

sections.source_quote: The control functions and/or external assurance providers, must, have the capacity to independently review and provide objective assurance of compliance with all IT-related activities as outlined in the financial institution's policies and procedures as well as with external requirements.

sections.heading: Notification and reporting requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must notify the responsible authority of material IT incidents within a determined timeframe after classification.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to notify the responsible authority of material IT incidents within a determined timeframe after classification.

requirements.text: A financial institution must notify the responsible authority of the financial sector law in terms of which the financial institution is licensed or registered, in the form and manner determined by the Authorities, of any systems failure, malfunction, delay or other disruptive event, within the determined timeframe, after classifying the event as a material incident.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must notify the responsible authority of the financial sector law in terms of which the financial institution is licensed or registered, in the form and manner determined by the Authorities, of any systems failure, malfunction, delay or other disruptive event, within the determined timeframe, after classifying the event as a material incident.

sections.heading: Short-title
sections.summary: Joint Standard is titled 'IT Governance and Risk Management for Financial Institutions, 2023'.
sections.source_quote: This Joint Standard is called ‘IT Governance and Risk Management for Financial Institutions, 2.023'.
